cards.text: {{c1::Member functions}} are functions defined inside a class definition that allow class objects to perform behaviors.

To define a member function inside a class:
class MyClass
{
public:
    {{c1::void}} functionName() {{c2::{}}}
};

cards.front: How do you call a member function on a class object?
cards.back:
Use the member selection operator: objectName.functionName();
Example: employee.print();

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.code:
class Point
{
public:
    int x{3};
    void print() { std::cout << x; }
};
Point p{};
p.print();
cards.explanation: Inside member functions, you can directly access data members of the implicit object (the object the function is called on). Here, x refers to p.x.

cards.text: When a member function is called, the object before the {{c1::.}} operator is called the {{c2::implicit object}}. Inside the member function, all member variables refer to those of the implicit object.

cards.concept: Why define member functions outside the class (with scope resolution)
cards.explanation: Defining member functions outside the class keeps the class definition concise and readable, showing the interface without implementation details. It also allows you to put the class definition in a header and the implementation in a .cpp file.
examples: // In class: void print();  // declaration only

cards.text:
To define a member function outside the class:
ReturnType {{c1::ClassName::}}functionName()
{
    // implementation
}

cards.back: Used to specify that a function belongs to a particular class or namespace

cards.front: What are the two ways to define a member function?
cards.back:
• Inside the class definition (implicit inline)
  • Outside the class definition using scope resolution operator (ClassName::)

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Missing scope resolution operator. Should be: void Date::print()
cards.explanation: Without Date::, this defines a non-member function named print, not the member function of class Date.

cards.back: Yes, member functions can have parameters just like normal functions. They can also have return values, default parameters, and be overloaded.

cards.text: Member functions defined inside the class definition are implicitly {{c1::inline}}. To make an outside member function inline, use the {{c2::inline}} keyword before the return type.
